the 5 best pre-seed VC funds in europe

I scout AI deals for a16z and cofounded an angel syndicate, so pre-seed is my whole world. these are the funds that wrote the first checks into europe's biggest companies, years before the US showed up.

1. Concept Ventures (London).
europe's largest dedicated pre-seed fund. they pre-seeded elevenlabs before anyone. fund II closed at $ 88M in 2025, 80% from US institutions. the americans are paying europeans to find european founders first. I'm good mates with Partner Oliver Kicks

2. Seedcamp (London).
reshma sohoni and carlos espinal have been doing this since 2007. they pre-seeded revolut, wise, and uipath. over 550 companies backed. the closest thing europe has to a seed-stage institution.

3. LocalGlobe / Phoenix Court (London).
saul and robin klein, father and son. wise, monzo, mistral, citymapper. they call themselves unicorn collectors and the cap tables back it up. checks from £100k.

4. Point Nine (Berlin).
christoph janz wrote the playbook for european B2B SaaS. zendesk, loom (sold to atlassian for almost $1B), typeform, revolut. if you're building software for businesses, this is the first call.

5. Kima Ventures (Paris).
xavier niel's fund, run by jean de la rochebrochard. €150k flat ticket, around 100 deals a year, 2 a week. the most active early-stage investor on earth. notion, alan, ledger.

london, berlin, paris. the first money into europe's unicorns is european.

4 startups from YC's wildest batch yet

I scout for a16z and run growth at a YC startup, so I read every batch debrief. W26 was the densest yet: 14 companies past $1M ARR before demo day, three times the last batch.

Save this if you are raising or hiring off this cohort.

1. Pocket: the revenue freak of the batch
A $129 clip-on mic that records and summarizes your meetings offline, plus a $15/month tier. They shipped 30,000 units in five months at roughly $27M ARR, growing 50% month over month. Co-founder Gabriel Dymowski is Polish, ex-DoxyChain, and proved hardware with a subscription attached is alive. I use it - it's nice

2. Ndea: the French bet against scaling
Francois Chollet built Keras and the ARC-AGI test, then started a $43M lab to chase real reasoning through program synthesis instead of stacking more parameters. The most contrarian company in the batch. If he is right, half the current AI roadmap is pointed at the wrong wall.

3. Beacon Health: boring vertical, real money
$5.4M seed led by Accel with a Sequoia scout in. They drop AI agents straight into electronic health records to run prior auth, screenings, and coding for primary care clinics. No chatbot, just workflows that used to need three people. The dull verticals keep paying the best.

4. Synthetic Sciences: 18 and shipping
Founders barely out of high school raised around $1.9M pre demo day for what they call Claude Code for science: agents that plan and run research pipelines for life-science teams. Youngest founders, hardest domain. That combination used to be a red flag. In this batch it was the whole thesis.

if you want to talk through which of these to copy for your own startup, add me on linkedin.

I ran growth at YC’s largest seed-funded startup ever, and here’s the five-step playbook that we were running.

Automate your weekly brief every Monday at 7:00 am. Your agent harness (sauna) pulls from Posthog, Stripe, and your CRM. There’s no reason you should be getting your information from different tools; it should all be collated by an AI agent and be given to you every morning before stand-up so that the meeting can be about decisions rather than data recap.
Build an outreach loop that doesn’t need you to monitor competitors. You should have an AI agent that surfaces anything worth noting from competitors in the last week: new launches, pricing changes, messaging shifts, customer wins, hiring patterns, funding announcements, and high-performing content. Instead of manually checking dozens of sources, you receive a concise digest with only the developments that may impact your positioning or pipeline.
Build a content pipeline that does not stall. It generates ideas and keeps you accountable to post and to shoot, edit, and post the content on a daily basis. The system should continuously turn customer conversations, product updates, objections, wins, and industry news into draft posts, video concepts, hooks, and talking points so you’re never staring at a blank page wondering what to publish.
Close the experiment loop. You should have all your data constantly fed to an AI agent that analyzes and tells you what worked, what didn’t, and what can be refined. Instead of reporting metrics, it identifies patterns: which channels produce qualified users, which creatives drive activation, which landing pages convert best, and where drop-offs are occurring so every experiment compounds into the next one.

That whole setup takes about an afternoon. You need a GTM engineer with good taste, and after that, most of your GTM runs on its own, servicing the really important parts for you to just act on.

From scouting deals for a16z, here's what separates founders who close in 2 weeks from the ones who spend 6 months pitching.

Save this before your next raise.

1. You've monopolized a tiny market nobody cares about yet.

Peter Thiel wrote this in Zero to One and most founders still get it backwards. We're targeting 1% of a $100B market is a soft pass. The founders I've seen raise effortlessly owned something small completely first. The only AI tool for compliance teams in EMEA. The only hiring tool for European founders raising in the US. VCs fund last movers in small markets, not first movers in large ones.

2. Your growth rate tells a better story than your ARR.

Burn multiple below 1.4x and 15%+ month-over-month growth and VCs are fighting over your round in 2026. The round I saw close in 9 days: zero to $1.2M ARR in 11 months, 90% net revenue retention, burn under 1.2x. Nobody asked about competition. Fundamental Tech, one of my portcos, went from pre-seed to a Series A on this playbook. Trajectory beats snapshot every time.

3. Smart angels are already in before you start the process.

Three $25K angel checks committed before you open the round is a great signal. Each check is someone saying they've seen hundreds of startups and this one is different. VCs call those angels after the first meeting. That call does half your selling for you. Ben Horowitz writes about this in HTATH. Social proof from people who have nothing to gain except returns.

What metrics do you actually need to raise a pre-seed, seed, or Series A in 2026?

I scout for a16z and run a GTM-engine advisory firm for startups, so this is just what I'm seeing in my own dealflow. It's subjective, everyone will tell you something different. It's also skewed towards strong performers in AI-native verticals.

Save this if you're raising in the next 12 months.

First thing to know: the goalposts moved. Vibe coding and vibe doing let a tiny team ship real product fast, so investors now expect traction earlier than they used to.

1. Pre-seed: traction is the new pedigree

Unless you've got a serious resume (repeat founder, ex-OpenAI, previously backed), team and narrative alone won't get you there anymore. I'm seeing pre-seed rounds "priced" off 10-50k MRR. 

2. Seed: this one barely moved imo

$1-2M ARR is still roughly the mark. It's the most stable checkpoint of the three. If you're here with clean retention, you're in a strong spot, the new paradigm hasn't inflated this number much.

3. Series A: $10M ARR and climbing

The number I keep hearing is $10M ARR. The acceleration in the months right before you raise matters as much as the figure itself. A flat $10M reads worse than a $6M ripping 20% month over month into the round. At 11x, we began raising our Series A at $3M run-rate, and had closed it at $4M forty days later.

real signal: How fast you got here

Caveat, this is my dealflow. It also shifts by sector and geography. I ran my data through carta - it seems like what i'm seeing is the top of the market (95% percentile), not the median, but then you know what it takes to raise from the likes of a16z, Benchmark, Sequoia etc.

The West Harbor of San Francisco sits on an engineered Marina shoreline built out from a former cove and beach/dune system.

The area was heavily filled before the 1915 Panama-Pacific International Exposition, and the exposition left behind a harbor.

Waves and currents push sand along the waterfront; although the breakwaters calm the water for boats, the calmer water also lets sand fall out and build sand banks. Over time, sand collects at the entrance, narrowing the channel, which accelerates the currents through the remaining gap, especially at low tide.

The only solution is dredging which happens approximately every year. tens of thousands of cubic yards of sand are excavated out of the harbor entrance, put onto a barge and then dumped in the middle of the bay.

This costs almost $1m and is done every year or two. #sanfrancisco #boat #marina

In older city restaurants, the most important piece of equipment might not be in the kitchen,  it is actually hidden under the sidewalk.

These are called sidewalk elevators or basement freight lifts, and they move food deliveries, kegs, supplies, trash bins, and other heavy loads between street level and basement storage.

They became common in dense urban buildings where restaurants had basements but no loading docks, rear alleys, or easy service entrances. Instead of hauling everything down narrow stairs, staff can open a sidewalk hatch, raise the platform to street level, load it, and send goods directly below.

Mechanically, many are hydraulic: an electric motor powers a pump, hydraulic fluid pushes a cylinder, and the platform rises. Others use electric/mechanical systems with chains, cables, screws, or hoists. Either way, the job is simple: move heavy stuff vertically in a building that was never designed for modern restaurant logistics.

A typical setup includes a steel platform, guide rails, sidewalk hatch doors, basement doors, controls, safety interlocks, drainage, and weatherproofing. The safety systems matter because this is a heavy machine operating through a public sidewalk. If a hatch, lock, interlock, or platform fails, the liability can be serious.

They are most common in older commercial districts in cities like New York, Chicago, Boston, Philadelphia, and San Francisco, where basements are part of daily operations. Restaurants use them for incoming food and outgoing trash. Bars use them for kegs. Hotels, stores, and warehouses use them for freight.

Costs vary widely. A repair might be a few thousand dollars, while a full replacement or new installation can reach tens of thousands — and complicated city retrofits can push into six figures once permits, sidewalk work, electrical upgrades, waterproofing, inspections, and structural changes are included.

The point is that this is invisible infrastructure. Customers never notice it. Most pedestrians walk right over it. But for a restaurant packed into an old urban building, a sidewalk elevator can be the difference between smooth operations and total chaos. #foodtiktok #foodie #city #work

He wrote these in 2013. Still relevant imo

1. Work on something that changes humanity

Sustainable energy. The internet. Space. Pick a thread big enough that every project connects to it. If your roadmap doesn't scare you, it's too small.

2. Speed is everything

He deferred his PhD in 1995 to start Zip2. Sold it. Started PayPal. The window for any new technology is shorter than you think and the winners are the ones who moved first, not the ones who planned hardest.

3. Think 10x, not 10%

SpaceX isn't a better rocket company. It's a plan to put life on Mars. Pick the goal that makes VCs uncomfortable, then work backwards. It's gotta be 10x better or 10x cheaper.

4. Determination is the moat

Tesla almost died. He put every dollar he had back in. The founders who survive aren't the smartest, they're the ones who refuse to flinch when the runway hits 3 months.

5. Live in SF (or california)

Still true. The well of talent, capital, and weird high-agency people in a 2 kilometer radius of SOMA is the single biggest unfair advantage on earth. Every founder I meet who moved here says the same thing: they wish they'd done it sooner.

6. Don't believe your own hype

He launched a wheel of cheese to orbit as a Monty Python joke. You have to not let things get to your head.

The list is 12 years old. ye nah still relevent.

California sea lions are intelligent, loud, social “eared seals” native to the West Coast of North America. 

They haul out on beaches, docks, buoys, jetties, and marina structures, and they are common around California harbors. 

Adult females are usually about 6 ft and 200–250 lb; adult males are much bigger, about 7–7.5 ft and often 600–850 lb. 

They live roughly 20–30 years. Pups are born dark brown, usually in early summer, weigh about 13–20 lb, and may stay with their mother for about a year while nursing and learning to swim and forage. 

The are opportunistic eaters: mainly schooling fish and squid, including anchovies, sardines, mackerel, rockfish, herring, squid, and octopus. 

They are known to steal or take fish from sport-fishing lines, commercial fishing gear, and fish passage facilities. 

They are not “man-eaters,” but they absolutely can bite: adult sea lions are large predators with strong jaws, and NOAA warns they can injure humans or pets, especially if provoked, fed, crowded, cornered, or habituated around boats and piers. 

The practical rule is simple: don’t feed them, don’t try to pet or swim with them, keep dogs leashed, and stay about 50 yards away from seals and sea lions when viewing wildlife.

Do you think fundraising skills are relevant for business success?

One of the biggest green flags VCs look for is a founder’s ability to raise capital. Domain expertise and sales can be hired over time. Fundraising can’t.

One nuance: founders can hire advisors, bankers (later stage), or finance help, but in early-stage venture investing, investors almost always want conviction in the founders themselves. The founder is expected to build investor relationships, tell the story, and close the round. That’s why fundraising ability is often treated as a core founder competency rather than something to delegate.

SpaceX paid $60B for a coding app this week - why?

I'm in growth & sales, not engineering. I can't write production code to save my life. I still ship thousands of lines of code per month and build apps & systems that work and scale (e.g. my viral reddit commenter).

If you watch where the money actually runs these days, it goes into the tools that let people like me build without an engineer. Here are the 5 names that matter.

1. The $60B one. Cursor.
Michael Truell started it with three MIT classmates in 2022. This month SpaceX agreed to buy it for $60B in stock, the biggest startup acquisition ever. An app that writes code, now worth more than most banks.

2. The robot engineer. Cognition.
Scott Wu won three programming olympiad golds, then built Devin, an AI that takes a ticket and ships the feature like a junior dev. ~$26B after a round in May. He hires the best human coders to build the thing that competes with human coders.

3. Build it in the browser. Replit.
Amjad Masad turned a coding playground into a $9B company on track for $1B in revenue. You describe the app, it builds and hosts it, no laptop setup, no engineer in the room.

4. Europe's shot. Lovable.
Anton Osika runs it out of Stockholm. ~$500M in revenue with around 146 people, reported in talks to raise at $12B. He keeps saying European founders don't need to move to San Francisco to win. Is he right? I hope he is.

5. The one that got eaten. Windsurf.
It was a top coding tool until Cognition bought it in 2025. Proof that in this race even the winners get swallowed before they get big.

Every guidance counselor spent a decade telling kids to learn to code. The tools above just made that advice age like milk.

Would you still tell a 16 year old to learn to code today?

I scout for a16z - arguably the most helpful fund in existence. But what does it really mean? Most founders think they're getting "VC money" when a scout writes a check. Nope - the mechanics are a bit stranger.

Here's how it actually runs:

One person signs off, on their own

There's no investment committee for a scout check. The scout backs you with their own conviction, which is why they can move in days while the fund itself takes weeks. I met Sohran after he DM'd me on IG - we went for a walk, and I invested the same day - first cheque. He's now gotten into a16z Speedrun.

The check is small, the signal is the whole point

The dollars are tiny next to a real round. What you're buying as a founder is a name in a big fund's system, attached to someone whose judgement they trust. That foot in the door is the actual asset. The scouts are also incredibly useful people in their own rights - e.g. I have scout friends at OpenAI, Anthropic, xAI and other great co's

Scouts are carry-aligned, so they're picky

A scout only makes money if the company works years later. So they back founders they'd happily defend in a room full of partners. Anyone writing you a scout check is putting their own track record behind you. I backed Fundamental at first round when the company was valued at $50M - it's now worth $1.2B -> nice

It's a farm system for the Series A (and sometimes seed)

Scout deals get watched. Scouts help founders build a relationship with the fund they scout for. The fund is quietly buying an option to lead your next round once you've proven something. Getting scouted is the audition for the real money. I invested in Ariel Harmoko, then invited him to a dinner with a16z partners, they decided to back him right after.

Founder conviction moves first, metrics second

The yes usually comes from a direct read on the founder, long before the numbers slide makes sense.

The 7 best SF hacker Houses

Ranked by how useful they actually are. Screenshot this before your next SF trip.

It’s no secret that many great entrepreneurs started off in smelly hacker houses. Nowadays they’re more like nice mansions, but the concept is the same: land with a duffle bag and laptop, live with a dozen hackers, spend your day tinkering.

Housing sorted, socializing sorted, and you lock in because everyone around you does. The houses become networking hubs. You come out launching your startup or joining your friend’s venture.

Best way to crash land in SF. That’s what I did - lived with 4 dudes in a nice house (see pics) - some of the happiest days of my life.

The informal ones are best, but for permanent residencies:

1. AGI House (Hillsborough) — you don’t apply, you get invited. Show up to their hackathons first.
2. The Embassy (PacHeights) — my friend Helena runs a $10M house for European founders. Brings VCs and big tech entrepreneurs to meet guests. Lmk if you want an intro.
3. HF0 — 12 weeks, free rent, $500k SAFE at the end. Apply with a co-founder and repo, not a deck.
4. Genesis House — AI-native, invite-only. Closest thing to a YC house outside batch.
5. Solaris (SOMA) — rolling residency, dense dinner calendar. Break in via friend-of-friend intro on a Tuesday night.
6. Frontier Tower — 16-floor vertical hacker city. Cheapest way into the scene. Old and smelly, but cheap af.
7. Sundai Club / Cerebral Valley — house-of-the-week energy. Follow the demo days, the houses follow you.
8. The best: the one your friend lives in — every real hacker house was someone’s group chat first.

Don’t move to SF for the houses. Move for the group chat that runs them.

5 VC funds that write half the checks in Silicon Valley

I scout for one of these.

Save this before your next fundraise.

1. a16z ($90B). the full-stack firm.
Raised $15B in January 2026, the largest single VC haul in history. On top of the check: a PR team, in-house recruiters, a talent exchange, a crypto platform, GMT advisory. I’m one of their scouts. The bar is genuinely high.

2. Sequoia ($56B). the longest track record.
Apple, Google, WhatsApp, Stripe. 60 years of picking. Just raised $7B in April 2026 for late-stage AI under Alfred Lin and Pat Grady after Roelof Botha stepped back. They bet on concentration. One great company beats ten decent ones.

3. Benchmark ($9.4B). intentionally that small.
I spent weeks working from their offices in SF. No associates, no scouts, no growth fund. Five equal partners, every check written by a partner personally. Backed Uber, Twitter, Discord, Snap. Bill Gurley sat on Uber’s board himself. That’s the whole model.

4. Founders Fund ($22B). the contrarian shop.
Peter Thiel’s firm just closed a $6B growth fund this month. SpaceX, Palantir, Anduril, Facebook (early). Thiel’s thesis is that technologies that should exist haven’t been built yet. What metrics do you actually need to raise a pre-seed, seed, or Series A in 2026?

I scout for a16z and run a GTM-engine advisory firm for startups, so this is just what I’m seeing in my own dealflow. It’s subjective, everyone will tell you something different. It’s also skewed towards strong performers in AI-native verticals.

Save this if you’re raising in the next 12 months.

First thing to know: the goalposts moved. Vibe coding and vibe doing let a tiny team ship real product fast, so investors now expect traction earlier than they used to.

1. Pre-seed: traction is the new pedigree

Unless you’ve got a serious resume (repeat founder, ex-OpenAI, previously backed), team and narrative alone won’t get you there anymore. I’m seeing pre-seed rounds “priced” off 10-50k MRR.

2. Seed: this one barely moved imo

$1-2M ARR is still roughly the mark. It’s the most stable checkpoint of the three. If you’re here with clean retention, you’re in a strong spot, the new paradigm hasn’t inflated this number much.

3. Series A: $10M ARR and climbing

The number I keep hearing is $10M ARR. The acceleration in the months right before you raise matters as much as the figure itself. A flat $10M reads worse than a $6M ripping 20% month over month into the round. At 11x, we began raising our Series A at $3M run-rate, and had closed it at $4M forty days later.

real signal: How fast you got here

Caveat, this is my dealflow. It also shifts by sector and geography. I ran my data through carta - it seems like what i’m seeing is the top of the market (95% percentile), not the median, but then you know what it takes to raise from the likes of a16z, Benchmark, Sequoia etc.

When should you actually raise a round?

I angel invest and scout for a16z, so this is just the pattern I keep seeing on the deals that close fast versus the ones that grind.

The mistake almost everyone makes: they wait until the runway gets scary, then start raising with 4 months of cash left. By then you're negotiating from weakness and every VC can smell it.

Raise when you don't need the money

The best rounds get done by founders who could walk away from every term sheet. When you have 12+ months of runway and you're not desperate, you set the pace. Desperation is the most expensive thing on your cap table.

Raise on a milestone you just hit, not one you're chasing

The right moment is right after a number moves. A launch lands, retention turns a corner, growth steps up. You raise on the proof, while the chart still points up and to the right. Investors fund the slope they can already see. E.g. one of my portfolio companies raising off the anouncement of a massive partnership right now.

The momentum is the pitch

A round opened off a fresh win lets you sell trajectory instead of explaining a plan. "We just did X, here's what the next 18 months unlock" beats "we'll hit X if you fund us." One is evidence, the other is hope.

So the timing rule is simple: lots of runway, a milestone freshly behind you, no pressure to say yes. Raise from strength or don't raise yet.

Caveat, this is what I see in my dealflow, not gospel. Capital-hungry or seasonal businesses play it differently.

5 startup terms every founder should know.

1. Churn
The rate at which customers cancel or stop using a product. High churn kills growth even when acquisition numbers look strong.

2. Burn Rate
How much cash your company spends each month beyond what it brings in. When burn is high and revenue is low, the clock runs faster.

3. Runway
How many months a company can operate before it runs out of money. Calculated by dividing how much cash is in the bank by the monthly burn rate.

4. Traction
Measurable evidence that a product is gaining market adoption. Three core signals are ARR, DAU and retention, with the most relevant signal shifting depending on stage.

5. Product-Market Fit
The point where a product satisfies a strong market demand. Often described as when growth starts pulling itself rather than being pushed.

Know these cold before your next investor meeting.

the larger your audience, the easier your life gets

founder content beats a $50k/mo ad budget - you gotta post.

From scouting for a16z, I keep watching founders pour money into ads while the cheapest distribution they own sits untouched. it's their own name.

srsly the larger your audience, the easier your life and business

1. Paid CAC keeps climbing, content keeps compounding.

The median SaaS company now spends close to $2 in sales and marketing to win $1 of new ARR, and Google ad costs rose again last year. A post you wrote in 2024 still pulls inbound today. an ad stops working the second you stop paying.

2. Your face out-reaches your logo.

Personal profiles pull several times the impressions of company pages for the same words. Adam Robinson scaled RB2B into the millions of ARR posting daily on LinkedIn with no venture money. people follow people, they scroll past brand accounts.

3. Build in public until strangers start selling for you.

Damon Chen ran PDF.ai and Testimonial.to where Twitter drove most of his early signups. he just posted his revenue, his code, his mistakes. Andrew Gazdecki of Acquire.com found him in a feed, not in an ad slot.

4. Distribution is the moat, content is how you build it.

In Zero to One, Peter Thiel argues weak distribution kills more startups than weak product. Justin Welsh runs an eight-figure business almost solo because his posts are the entire funnel. lemlist crossed serious ARR spending a fraction of what venture-backed rivals burn on ads (see my video about meeting Guillaume Moubeche, founder of Lemlist).

the price of an audience is your time and a year of looking dumb in public. the price of ads is forever.
if anything, just run ads on your best performing content.

3 types of posts to generate enterprise inbound#
I ran growth at a YC startup, and I posted almost every day. The founders who win inbound treat content like pipeline, not a vanity feed.

Save this before you write your next post.

Your funding announcement is the biggest inbound campaign you'll ever run.

I was employee #6 at 11x - we reaised $21M from Benchmark, and then $50M from a16z - these became the 2 single most effective pipeline generation events we ever had. These funds have distribution, but they're also a testament to the trust they place on you. For us, it drove over $5M in pipeline.

Give away the system, not the highlight reel.

Tommy Clark built Compound Content Studio to seven figures with 100% inbound. He posts the exact client systems and raw agency numbers most people keep hidden. Buyers are tired of opinion. Show them operational proof and the reach-outs start on their own.

Build in public before anyone in your category does.

Maor Shlomo posted Base44's revenue, churn and product calls on LinkedIn, a platform most indie builders ignored. He went from zero to $1M ARR in weeks and sold to Wix for around $80M. Honesty over corporate polish shortens the path to a buyer. Pierre-Louis Biojout from NanoCorp does the same, and it works like magic.

Every post is a sales call.

Ben Horowitz's whole argument in his main book is that the CEO never stops selling the story. Content is that job at scale. Reelful (Kate Deyneka), is built on exactly this behavior, founders turning attention into pipeline.

don't post to look smart. Instead post like it's a sales call.

why play this snake and fruit game or puzzles in general?

on the surface they’re pretty simple. move things around, clear a level, find a path, don’t mess up. but the longer you play them the more you realize they’re teaching a lot of the same skills you use in real life

they make you slow down and actually think instead of reacting right away. you start looking at the bigger picture and asking yourself what happens if i do this first? what does this open up? am i about to make things harder for myself?

they’re also great for problem solving. you get stuck, try something, fail, reset, try again. over and over. and honestly that’s how most real problems get solved too

they help with focus because for a few minutes it’s just you and the puzzle. no scrolling, no multitasking, just one thing that needs figuring out

and a lot of the time the obvious answer isn’t the right one. puzzle games kind of force you to look at things differently and be willing to change your approach when something isn’t working

they also make failure feel a lot less dramatic. every mistake gives you more information. you learn something, adjust, and keep going

so yeah maybe i’m just playing a weird little snakes and fruit game but i’m also practicing patience, strategy, focus, and how to think through problems one level at a time #puzzle

they help with focus because for a few minutes it’s just you and the puzzle. no scrolling, no multitasking, just one thing that needs figuring out

He almost fumbled his billionaire client, but instead he closed the deal and proved himself to be a legend 

Kurt Rappaport’s career has always been built on one thing: being more prepared, more relentless, and more willing to move than everyone else.

The story that explains him best starts two decades ago at 10:30 p.m., when his phone rang with no caller ID. It was Larry Ellison.

Ellison told him he was flying into Santa Monica the next morning and wanted to see a house on Carbon Beach, near his friend David Geffen. Rappaport instantly had the property in mind: a Malibu listing on PCH. The problem was, it wasn’t his listing.

He tried reaching the listing agent that night. No answer. The next morning, he got her on the phone, but she pushed back and asked if “Barry” could come another day. Rappaport corrected her — “First off, it’s Larry, not Barry” — and made it clear Ellison was coming that morning.

When she still said no, Rappaport went to the house anyway, got inside, showed Ellison the property at 10 a.m., and had the deal put together by lunch.

That moment became the blueprint for his career.

Rappaport wasn’t just trying to be another Beverly Hills broker. He grew up around Hollywood power players, spent nights in Robert Evans’ screening room, read the L.A. Times “Hot Property” column religiously, and memorized addresses, prices, and square footage the way other kids memorized baseball stats.

By 19, he had left USC, left William Morris, and gotten his real estate license. By 22, he was already representing names like Heidi Fleiss. By 1999, he and Stephen Shapiro launched Westside Estate Agency, with a simple division of labor: Shapiro managed, Rappaport sold.

And sell he did.

He became one of California’s most dominant luxury agents, representing clients like Larry Ellison, David Geffen, Jeffrey Katzenberg, Ellen DeGeneres, Tom Brady, Larry King, and Antonio Villaraigosa. The Real Deal ranked him first among L.A. agents by sales volume in 2018 and 2019, with $627 million and $513 million in deals, respectively.

Some of his biggest deals were off-market trophy transactions, including Jeffrey Katzenberg’s $125 million Beverly Hills sale to WhatsApp co-founder Jan Koum, one of the largest single-family home sales in California history.

But what makes Rappaport interesting isn’t just the sales volume. It’s the mindset.

He sees real estate less as sales and more as strategy, taste, timing, access, and power. To him, a great house is not just a house. It is art, leverage, identity, and sometimes a business card.

That ambition has also pushed him beyond brokerage. He has bought, built, and sold trophy properties of his own across Beverly Hills, Bel Air, Malibu, and Brentwood. He has talked openly about wanting to own a Major League Baseball team. He even explored buying the New York Mets before Steve Cohen ultimately bought the franchise for $2.4 billion.

Rappaport’s story is really about proximity to power — and the tension of wanting not just to serve the ultra-wealthy, but to become one of them.

He built his career by knowing every house, every buyer, every angle, and every move before anyone else. And even after decades at the top of luxury real estate, the same instinct still seems to drive him:

Francois Chollet built Keras and the ARC-AGI test, then started a $43M lab to chase real reasoning through program synthesis instead of stacking more parameters. The most contrarian company in the batch. If he is right, half the current AI roadmap is pointed at the wrong wall.

$5.4M seed led by Accel with a Sequoia scout in. They drop AI agents straight into electronic health records to run prior auth, screenings, and coding for primary care clinics. No chatbot, just workflows that used to need three people. The dull verticals keep paying the best.

Founders barely out of high school raised around $1.9M pre demo day for what they call Claude Code for science: agents that plan and run research pipelines for life-science teams. Youngest founders, hardest domain. That combination used to be a red flag. In this batch it was the whole thesis.

I ran growth at YC’s largest seed-funded startup ever, and here’s the five-step playbook that we were running.

Automate your weekly brief every Monday at 7:00 am. Your agent harness (sauna) pulls from Posthog, Stripe, and your CRM. There’s no reason you should be getting your information from different tools; it should all be collated by an AI agent and be given to you every morning before stand-up so that the meeting can be about decisions rather than data recap.
Build an outreach loop that doesn’t need you to monitor competitors. You should have an AI agent that surfaces anything worth noting from competitors in the last week: new launches, pricing changes, messaging shifts, customer wins, hiring patterns, funding announcements, and high-performing content. Instead of manually checking dozens of sources, you receive a concise digest with only the developments that may impact your positioning or pipeline.
Build a content pipeline that does not stall. It generates ideas and keeps you accountable to post and to shoot, edit, and post the content on a daily basis. The system should continuously turn customer conversations, product updates, objections, wins, and industry news into draft posts, video concepts, hooks, and talking points so you’re never staring at a blank page wondering what to publish.
Close the experiment loop. You should have all your data constantly fed to an AI agent that analyzes and tells you what worked, what didn’t, and what can be refined. Instead of reporting metrics, it identifies patterns: which channels produce qualified users, which creatives drive activation, which landing pages convert best, and where drop-offs are occurring so every experiment compounds into the next one.

That whole setup takes about an afternoon. You need a GTM engineer with good taste, and after that, most of your GTM runs on its own, servicing the really important parts for you to just act on.

From scouting deals for a16z, here's what separates founders who close in 2 weeks from the ones who spend 6 months pitching.

Save this before your next raise.

1. You've monopolized a tiny market nobody cares about yet.

Peter Thiel wrote this in Zero to One and most founders still get it backwards. We're targeting 1% of a $100B market is a soft pass. The founders I've seen raise effortlessly owned something small completely first. The only AI tool for compliance teams in EMEA. The only hiring tool for European founders raising in the US. VCs fund last movers in small markets, not first movers in large ones.

2. Your growth rate tells a better story than your ARR.

Burn multiple below 1.4x and 15%+ month-over-month growth and VCs are fighting over your round in 2026. The round I saw close in 9 days: zero to $1.2M ARR in 11 months, 90% net revenue retention, burn under 1.2x. Nobody asked about competition. Fundamental Tech, one of my portcos, went from pre-seed to a Series A on this playbook. Trajectory beats snapshot every time.

3. Smart angels are already in before you start the process.

Three $25K angel checks committed before you open the round is a great signal. Each check is someone saying they've seen hundreds of startups and this one is different. VCs call those angels after the first meeting. That call does half your selling for you. Ben Horowitz writes about this in HTATH. Social proof from people who have nothing to gain except returns.

I scout for a16z and run a GTM-engine advisory firm for startups, so this is just what I'm seeing in my own dealflow. It's subjective, everyone will tell you something different. It's also skewed towards strong performers in AI-native verticals.

First thing to know: the goalposts moved. Vibe coding and vibe doing let a tiny team ship real product fast, so investors now expect traction earlier than they used to.

1. Pre-seed: traction is the new pedigree

Unless you've got a serious resume (repeat founder, ex-OpenAI, previously backed), team and narrative alone won't get you there anymore. I'm seeing pre-seed rounds "priced" off 10-50k MRR. 

2. Seed: this one barely moved imo

$1-2M ARR is still roughly the mark. It's the most stable checkpoint of the three. If you're here with clean retention, you're in a strong spot, the new paradigm hasn't inflated this number much.

3. Series A: $10M ARR and climbing

The number I keep hearing is $10M ARR. The acceleration in the months right before you raise matters as much as the figure itself. A flat $10M reads worse than a $6M ripping 20% month over month into the round. At 11x, we began raising our Series A at $3M run-rate, and had closed it at $4M forty days later.

The West Harbor of San Francisco sits on an engineered Marina shoreline built out from a former cove and beach/dune system.

The area was heavily filled before the 1915 Panama-Pacific International Exposition, and the exposition left behind a harbor.

Waves and currents push sand along the waterfront; although the breakwaters calm the water for boats, the calmer water also lets sand fall out and build sand banks. Over time, sand collects at the entrance, narrowing the channel, which accelerates the currents through the remaining gap, especially at low tide.

The only solution is dredging which happens approximately every year. tens of thousands of cubic yards of sand are excavated out of the harbor entrance, put onto a barge and then dumped in the middle of the bay.

This costs almost $1m and is done every year or two. #sanfrancisco #boat #marina

In older city restaurants, the most important piece of equipment might not be in the kitchen,  it is actually hidden under the sidewalk.

These are called sidewalk elevators or basement freight lifts, and they move food deliveries, kegs, supplies, trash bins, and other heavy loads between street level and basement storage.

They became common in dense urban buildings where restaurants had basements but no loading docks, rear alleys, or easy service entrances. Instead of hauling everything down narrow stairs, staff can open a sidewalk hatch, raise the platform to street level, load it, and send goods directly below.

Mechanically, many are hydraulic: an electric motor powers a pump, hydraulic fluid pushes a cylinder, and the platform rises. Others use electric/mechanical systems with chains, cables, screws, or hoists. Either way, the job is simple: move heavy stuff vertically in a building that was never designed for modern restaurant logistics.

A typical setup includes a steel platform, guide rails, sidewalk hatch doors, basement doors, controls, safety interlocks, drainage, and weatherproofing. The safety systems matter because this is a heavy machine operating through a public sidewalk. If a hatch, lock, interlock, or platform fails, the liability can be serious.

They are most common in older commercial districts in cities like New York, Chicago, Boston, Philadelphia, and San Francisco, where basements are part of daily operations. Restaurants use them for incoming food and outgoing trash. Bars use them for kegs. Hotels, stores, and warehouses use them for freight.

Costs vary widely. A repair might be a few thousand dollars, while a full replacement or new installation can reach tens of thousands — and complicated city retrofits can push into six figures once permits, sidewalk work, electrical upgrades, waterproofing, inspections, and structural changes are included.

The point is that this is invisible infrastructure. Customers never notice it. Most pedestrians walk right over it. But for a restaurant packed into an old urban building, a sidewalk elevator can be the difference between smooth operations and total chaos. #foodtiktok #foodie #city #work

He wrote these in 2013. Still relevant imo

1. Work on something that changes humanity

Sustainable energy. The internet. Space. Pick a thread big enough that every project connects to it. If your roadmap doesn't scare you, it's too small.

2. Speed is everything

He deferred his PhD in 1995 to start Zip2. Sold it. Started PayPal. The window for any new technology is shorter than you think and the winners are the ones who moved first, not the ones who planned hardest.

3. Think 10x, not 10%

SpaceX isn't a better rocket company. It's a plan to put life on Mars. Pick the goal that makes VCs uncomfortable, then work backwards. It's gotta be 10x better or 10x cheaper.

4. Determination is the moat

Tesla almost died. He put every dollar he had back in. The founders who survive aren't the smartest, they're the ones who refuse to flinch when the runway hits 3 months.

5. Live in SF (or california)

Still true. The well of talent, capital, and weird high-agency people in a 2 kilometer radius of SOMA is the single biggest unfair advantage on earth. Every founder I meet who moved here says the same thing: they wish they'd done it sooner.

6. Don't believe your own hype

He launched a wheel of cheese to orbit as a Monty Python joke. You have to not let things get to your head.

California sea lions are intelligent, loud, social “eared seals” native to the West Coast of North America. 

They haul out on beaches, docks, buoys, jetties, and marina structures, and they are common around California harbors. 

Adult females are usually about 6 ft and 200–250 lb; adult males are much bigger, about 7–7.5 ft and often 600–850 lb. 

They live roughly 20–30 years. Pups are born dark brown, usually in early summer, weigh about 13–20 lb, and may stay with their mother for about a year while nursing and learning to swim and forage. 

The are opportunistic eaters: mainly schooling fish and squid, including anchovies, sardines, mackerel, rockfish, herring, squid, and octopus. 

They are known to steal or take fish from sport-fishing lines, commercial fishing gear, and fish passage facilities. 

They are not “man-eaters,” but they absolutely can bite: adult sea lions are large predators with strong jaws, and NOAA warns they can injure humans or pets, especially if provoked, fed, crowded, cornered, or habituated around boats and piers. 

The practical rule is simple: don’t feed them, don’t try to pet or swim with them, keep dogs leashed, and stay about 50 yards away from seals and sea lions when viewing wildlife.

I scout for a16z - arguably the most helpful fund in existence. But what does it really mean? Most founders think they're getting "VC money" when a scout writes a check. Nope - the mechanics are a bit stranger.

Here's how it actually runs:

One person signs off, on their own

There's no investment committee for a scout check. The scout backs you with their own conviction, which is why they can move in days while the fund itself takes weeks. I met Sohran after he DM'd me on IG - we went for a walk, and I invested the same day - first cheque. He's now gotten into a16z Speedrun.

The check is small, the signal is the whole point

The dollars are tiny next to a real round. What you're buying as a founder is a name in a big fund's system, attached to someone whose judgement they trust. That foot in the door is the actual asset. The scouts are also incredibly useful people in their own rights - e.g. I have scout friends at OpenAI, Anthropic, xAI and other great co's

Scouts are carry-aligned, so they're picky

A scout only makes money if the company works years later. So they back founders they'd happily defend in a room full of partners. Anyone writing you a scout check is putting their own track record behind you. I backed Fundamental at first round when the company was valued at $50M - it's now worth $1.2B -> nice

It's a farm system for the Series A (and sometimes seed)

Scout deals get watched. Scouts help founders build a relationship with the fund they scout for. The fund is quietly buying an option to lead your next round once you've proven something. Getting scouted is the audition for the real money. I invested in Ariel Harmoko, then invited him to a dinner with a16z partners, they decided to back him right after.

Founder conviction moves first, metrics second

The yes usually comes from a direct read on the founder, long before the numbers slide makes sense.

3. Series A: $10M ARR and climbing

The number I keep hearing is $10M ARR. The acceleration in the months right before you raise matters as much as the figure itself. A flat $10M reads worse than a $6M ripping 20% month over month into the round. At 11x, we began raising our Series A at $3M run-rate, and had closed it at $4M forty days later.

real signal: How fast you got here

In older city restaurants, the most important piece of equipment might not be in the kitchen,  it is actually hidden under the sidewalk.

These are called sidewalk elevators or basement freight lifts, and they move food deliveries, kegs, supplies, trash bins, and other heavy loads between street level and basement storage.

They became common in dense urban buildings where restaurants had basements but no loading docks, rear alleys, or easy service entrances. Instead of hauling everything down narrow stairs, staff can open a sidewalk hatch, raise the platform to street level, load it, and send goods directly below.

Mechanically, many are hydraulic: an electric motor powers a pump, hydraulic fluid pushes a cylinder, and the platform rises. Others use electric/mechanical systems with chains, cables, screws, or hoists. Either way, the job is simple: move heavy stuff vertically in a building that was never designed for modern restaurant logistics.

A typical setup includes a steel platform, guide rails, sidewalk hatch doors, basement doors, controls, safety interlocks, drainage, and weatherproofing. The safety systems matter because this is a heavy machine operating through a public sidewalk. If a hatch, lock, interlock, or platform fails, the liability can be serious.

They are most common in older commercial districts in cities like New York, Chicago, Boston, Philadelphia, and San Francisco, where basements are part of daily operations. Restaurants use them for incoming food and outgoing trash. Bars use them for kegs. Hotels, stores, and warehouses use them for freight.

Costs vary widely. A repair might be a few thousand dollars, while a full replacement or new installation can reach tens of thousands — and complicated city retrofits can push into six figures once permits, sidewalk work, electrical upgrades, waterproofing, inspections, and structural changes are included.

The point is that this is invisible infrastructure. Customers never notice it. Most pedestrians walk right over it. But for a restaurant packed into an old urban building, a sidewalk elevator can be the difference between smooth operations and total chaos. #foodtiktok #foodie #city #work

From scouting deals for a16z, here's what separates founders who close in 2 weeks from the ones who spend 6 months pitching.

Save this before your next raise.

1. You've monopolized a tiny market nobody cares about yet.

Peter Thiel wrote this in Zero to One and most founders still get it backwards. We're targeting 1% of a $100B market is a soft pass. The founders I've seen raise effortlessly owned something small completely first. The only AI tool for compliance teams in EMEA. The only hiring tool for European founders raising in the US. VCs fund last movers in small markets, not first movers in large ones.

2. Your growth rate tells a better story than your ARR.

Burn multiple below 1.4x and 15%+ month-over-month growth and VCs are fighting over your round in 2026. The round I saw close in 9 days: zero to $1.2M ARR in 11 months, 90% net revenue retention, burn under 1.2x. Nobody asked about competition. Fundamental Tech, one of my portcos, went from pre-seed to a Series A on this playbook. Trajectory beats snapshot every time.

3. Smart angels are already in before you start the process.

Three $25K angel checks committed before you open the round is a great signal. Each check is someone saying they've seen hundreds of startups and this one is different. VCs call those angels after the first meeting. That call does half your selling for you. Ben Horowitz writes about this in HTATH. Social proof from people who have nothing to gain except returns.

I ran growth at YC’s largest seed-funded startup ever, and here’s the five-step playbook that we were running.

Automate your weekly brief every Monday at 7:00 am. Your agent harness (sauna) pulls from Posthog, Stripe, and your CRM. There’s no reason you should be getting your information from different tools; it should all be collated by an AI agent and be given to you every morning before stand-up so that the meeting can be about decisions rather than data recap.
Build an outreach loop that doesn’t need you to monitor competitors. You should have an AI agent that surfaces anything worth noting from competitors in the last week: new launches, pricing changes, messaging shifts, customer wins, hiring patterns, funding announcements, and high-performing content. Instead of manually checking dozens of sources, you receive a concise digest with only the developments that may impact your positioning or pipeline.
Build a content pipeline that does not stall. It generates ideas and keeps you accountable to post and to shoot, edit, and post the content on a daily basis. The system should continuously turn customer conversations, product updates, objections, wins, and industry news into draft posts, video concepts, hooks, and talking points so you’re never staring at a blank page wondering what to publish.
Close the experiment loop. You should have all your data constantly fed to an AI agent that analyzes and tells you what worked, what didn’t, and what can be refined. Instead of reporting metrics, it identifies patterns: which channels produce qualified users, which creatives drive activation, which landing pages convert best, and where drop-offs are occurring so every experiment compounds into the next one.

That whole setup takes about an afternoon. You need a GTM engineer with good taste, and after that, most of your GTM runs on its own, servicing the really important parts for you to just act on.

$1-2M ARR is still roughly the mark. It's the most stable checkpoint of the three. If you're here with clean retention, you're in a strong spot, the new paradigm hasn't inflated this number much.

3. Series A: $10M ARR and climbing

The number I keep hearing is $10M ARR. The acceleration in the months right before you raise matters as much as the figure itself. A flat $10M reads worse than a $6M ripping 20% month over month into the round. At 11x, we began raising our Series A at $3M run-rate, and had closed it at $4M forty days later.

real signal: How fast you got here

Caveat, this is my dealflow. It also shifts by sector and geography. I ran my data through carta - it seems like what i'm seeing is the top of the market (95% percentile), not the median, but then you know what it takes to raise from the likes of a16z, Benchmark, Sequoia etc.

The West Harbor of San Francisco sits on an engineered Marina shoreline built out from a former cove and beach/dune system.

The area was heavily filled before the 1915 Panama-Pacific International Exposition, and the exposition left behind a harbor.

Waves and currents push sand along the waterfront; although the breakwaters calm the water for boats, the calmer water also lets sand fall out and build sand banks. Over time, sand collects at the entrance, narrowing the channel, which accelerates the currents through the remaining gap, especially at low tide.

The only solution is dredging which happens approximately every year. tens of thousands of cubic yards of sand are excavated out of the harbor entrance, put onto a barge and then dumped in the middle of the bay.

This costs almost $1m and is done every year or two. #sanfrancisco #boat #marina

And the reactive discourse seems to be, as per usual: a few people angered, a few more inspired, and the very many that say “yep, that sounds good, go ahead” - which I think is healthy in a democracy

Ninja method

When should you actually raise a round?

I angel invest and scout for a16z, so this is just the pattern I keep seeing on the deals that close fast versus the ones that grind.

The mistake almost everyone makes: they wait until the runway gets scary, then start raising with 4 months of cash left. By then you're negotiating from weakness and every VC can smell it.

Raise when you don't need the money

The best rounds get done by founders who could walk away from every term sheet. When you have 12+ months of runway and you're not desperate, you set the pace. Desperation is the most expensive thing on your cap table.

Raise on a milestone you just hit, not one you're chasing

The right moment is right after a number moves. A launch lands, retention turns a corner, growth steps up. You raise on the proof, while the chart still points up and to the right. Investors fund the slope they can already see. E.g. one of my portfolio companies raising off the anouncement of a massive partnership right now.

The momentum is the pitch

A round opened off a fresh win lets you sell trajectory instead of explaining a plan. "We just did X, here's what the next 18 months unlock" beats "we'll hit X if you fund us." One is evidence, the other is hope.

So the timing rule is simple: lots of runway, a milestone freshly behind you, no pressure to say yes. Raise from strength or don't raise yet.

Caveat, this is what I see in my dealflow, not gospel. Capital-hungry or seasonal businesses play it differently.

California sea lions are intelligent, loud, social “eared seals” native to the West Coast of North America. 

They haul out on beaches, docks, buoys, jetties, and marina structures, and they are common around California harbors. 

Adult females are usually about 6 ft and 200–250 lb; adult males are much bigger, about 7–7.5 ft and often 600–850 lb. 

They live roughly 20–30 years. Pups are born dark brown, usually in early summer, weigh about 13–20 lb, and may stay with their mother for about a year while nursing and learning to swim and forage. 

The are opportunistic eaters: mainly schooling fish and squid, including anchovies, sardines, mackerel, rockfish, herring, squid, and octopus. 

They are known to steal or take fish from sport-fishing lines, commercial fishing gear, and fish passage facilities. 

They are not “man-eaters,” but they absolutely can bite: adult sea lions are large predators with strong jaws, and NOAA warns they can injure humans or pets, especially if provoked, fed, crowded, cornered, or habituated around boats and piers. 

The practical rule is simple: don’t feed them, don’t try to pet or swim with them, keep dogs leashed, and stay about 50 yards away from seals and sea lions when viewing wildlife.

He wrote these in 2013. Still relevant imo

1. Work on something that changes humanity

Sustainable energy. The internet. Space. Pick a thread big enough that every project connects to it. If your roadmap doesn't scare you, it's too small.

2. Speed is everything

He deferred his PhD in 1995 to start Zip2. Sold it. Started PayPal. The window for any new technology is shorter than you think and the winners are the ones who moved first, not the ones who planned hardest.

3. Think 10x, not 10%

SpaceX isn't a better rocket company. It's a plan to put life on Mars. Pick the goal that makes VCs uncomfortable, then work backwards. It's gotta be 10x better or 10x cheaper.

4. Determination is the moat

Tesla almost died. He put every dollar he had back in. The founders who survive aren't the smartest, they're the ones who refuse to flinch when the runway hits 3 months.

5. Live in SF (or california)

Still true. The well of talent, capital, and weird high-agency people in a 2 kilometer radius of SOMA is the single biggest unfair advantage on earth. Every founder I meet who moved here says the same thing: they wish they'd done it sooner.

6. Don't believe your own hype

He launched a wheel of cheese to orbit as a Monty Python joke. You have to not let things get to your head.

I scout for a16z - arguably the most helpful fund in existence. But what does it really mean? Most founders think they're getting "VC money" when a scout writes a check. Nope - the mechanics are a bit stranger.

Here's how it actually runs:

One person signs off, on their own

There's no investment committee for a scout check. The scout backs you with their own conviction, which is why they can move in days while the fund itself takes weeks. I met Sohran after he DM'd me on IG - we went for a walk, and I invested the same day - first cheque. He's now gotten into a16z Speedrun.

The check is small, the signal is the whole point

The dollars are tiny next to a real round. What you're buying as a founder is a name in a big fund's system, attached to someone whose judgement they trust. That foot in the door is the actual asset. The scouts are also incredibly useful people in their own rights - e.g. I have scout friends at OpenAI, Anthropic, xAI and other great co's

Scouts are carry-aligned, so they're picky

A scout only makes money if the company works years later. So they back founders they'd happily defend in a room full of partners. Anyone writing you a scout check is putting their own track record behind you. I backed Fundamental at first round when the company was valued at $50M - it's now worth $1.2B -> nice

It's a farm system for the Series A (and sometimes seed)

Scout deals get watched. Scouts help founders build a relationship with the fund they scout for. The fund is quietly buying an option to lead your next round once you've proven something. Getting scouted is the audition for the real money. I invested in Ariel Harmoko, then invited him to a dinner with a16z partners, they decided to back him right after.

Founder conviction moves first, metrics second

The yes usually comes from a direct read on the founder, long before the numbers slide makes sense.

5 startup terms every founder should know.

1. Churn
The rate at which customers cancel or stop using a product. High churn kills growth even when acquisition numbers look strong.

2. Burn Rate
How much cash your company spends each month beyond what it brings in. When burn is high and revenue is low, the clock runs faster.

3. Runway
How many months a company can operate before it runs out of money. Calculated by dividing how much cash is in the bank by the monthly burn rate.

4. Traction
Measurable evidence that a product is gaining market adoption. Three core signals are ARR, DAU and retention, with the most relevant signal shifting depending on stage.

5. Product-Market Fit
The point where a product satisfies a strong market demand. Often described as when growth starts pulling itself rather than being pushed.

Know these cold before your next investor meeting.

The 7 best SF hacker Houses

Ranked by how useful they actually are. Screenshot this before your next SF trip.

It’s no secret that many great entrepreneurs started off in smelly hacker houses. Nowadays they’re more like nice mansions, but the concept is the same: land with a duffle bag and laptop, live with a dozen hackers, spend your day tinkering.

Housing sorted, socializing sorted, and you lock in because everyone around you does. The houses become networking hubs. You come out launching your startup or joining your friend’s venture.

Best way to crash land in SF. That’s what I did - lived with 4 dudes in a nice house (see pics) - some of the happiest days of my life.

The informal ones are best, but for permanent residencies:

1. AGI House (Hillsborough) — you don’t apply, you get invited. Show up to their hackathons first.
2. The Embassy (PacHeights) — my friend Helena runs a $10M house for European founders. Brings VCs and big tech entrepreneurs to meet guests. Lmk if you want an intro.
3. HF0 — 12 weeks, free rent, $500k SAFE at the end. Apply with a co-founder and repo, not a deck.
4. Genesis House — AI-native, invite-only. Closest thing to a YC house outside batch.
5. Solaris (SOMA) — rolling residency, dense dinner calendar. Break in via friend-of-friend intro on a Tuesday night.
6. Frontier Tower — 16-floor vertical hacker city. Cheapest way into the scene. Old and smelly, but cheap af.
7. Sundai Club / Cerebral Valley — house-of-the-week energy. Follow the demo days, the houses follow you.
8. The best: the one your friend lives in — every real hacker house was someone’s group chat first.

Don’t move to SF for the houses. Move for the group chat that runs them.
